rows.who: Will download a prebuilt binary and run it; will NOT clone, build from source, or read the docs. Spends seconds, not minutes.
rows.job: Download a binary and watch it deny a tool call in under a minute — no toolchain, no key, no GPU.

affordances.need: a CI job that cross-compiles and uploads prebuilt binaries to GitHub Releases
a curl|sh installer that fetches the right prebuilt binary and verifies its checksum
a no-toolchain download path on the front page (release archive or install.sh)
a copy-pasteable no-key/no-GPU first command in a fenced block
the honesty ledger backs the one-static-binary, no-dependencies claim
a package-manager install (Homebrew/winget/scoop) for the dev who will not even curl

gaps: No Homebrew/winget/scoop package yet — the lowest-effort install is curl|sh or a release archive
The single command runs a demo/preflight; a polished one-window TUI would lower the bar further

rows.who: Builds an app or agent and wants to point an existing AI harness (Claude Code, Cursor, an MCP client) at fak with no hand-wiring.
rows.job: Drop fak in front of my agent so every tool call is governed, using a config my harness already auto-loads.

affordances.need: an integration index that routes 'which agent do you run?' to a recipe
a drop-in .mcp.json that wires fak serve --stdio with no setup
a worked example with a real policy a developer can copy
a quickstart that gets from clone to a running kernel

gaps: Recipes cover Claude/Cursor/Codex/MCP; a brand-new harness still needs its own recipe written

rows.who: Embeds fak (the binary, fronting a model server) into a real service and needs a stable interface plus a way to extend it.
rows.job: Install fak into a service, front my model server, and depend on a frozen interface I can build a leaf onto.

affordances.need: the go install ...@latest one-liner (the module is at the repo root, so it resolves)
a CLI reference documenting every verb and flag
a config reference (flags, env vars, policy schema)
the frozen-ABI / layering discipline an integrator depends on for stability
the additive extension seams (Register* + a leaf) for building on fak without forking
the Go module declared at the repo root so the install path resolves directly

gaps: Integration is binary-first (front it, register a leaf); the internal/ packages are not a published importable Go API — by design (the spine is frozen)
